Both steps are done. Summary:

**Pushed:** `docs: template cleanup` landed on `main` via `vc-x1 push` (README trim, todo.md dummy-entry removal, plus a new chores-01 section with an empty `Commits:` line to backfill next push). The `.claude` finalize was launched alongside.

**Todo #1 picked up** as Preparation `0.3.0-0` (working copy, uncommitted):
- `notes/todo.md` — moved into `## In Progress` under the bold title **docs: zero-copy ring buffer design**, with this proposed ladder:
  - `0.3.0-0` pick up todo, open chores, bump version (current)
  - `0.3.0-1` requirements + constraints (no_std, zerocopy, IPC)
  - `0.3.0-2` API + memory-layout design doc
  - `0.3.0-3` prototype crate validating the design
  - `0.3.0` close-out and validation
- `notes/chores/chores-01.md` — opened `## docs: zero-copy ring buffer design` with an empty `Commits:` line.
- `version.toml` — bumped to `0.3.0-0`.

One judgment call to flag: I framed the cycle as *design-first* (`docs:` type, design doc as the deliverable, prototype crate in `-3`). If you'd rather this cycle go straight to a `feat:` implementation cycle, say so and I'll reshape the ladder.

Ready to commit — please review the working-copy diff and the ladder.

message.content: This looks good, let's multi-step it

content.text:
Understood — we'll run the ladder as separate commits, one review at each step. First up is committing this Preparation (`0.3.0-0`). Proposed description:

**Title:** `docs: pick up zero-copy ring buffer design`

**Body:**
```
Open the 0.3.0 cycle: Todo #1 moves to In Progress with a
four-step ladder (requirements, design doc, prototype,
close-out).

- notes/todo.md: move Todo #1 into In Progress with ladder
- notes/chores/chores-01.md: open 'docs: zero-copy ring
  buffer design' section with empty Commits: line
- version.toml: bump to 0.3.0-0
```

Two things to confirm:
1. Title is 42 chars, fine — but note the chores header stays `docs: zero-copy ring buffer design` (the anticipated *close-out* title), which is correct per protocol.
2. Push cadence: push each step as it lands (`vc-x1 push main` per commit, Keep separate shape), or commit locally and publish the whole cycle at close-out? Per-step pushing is the simpler default with the current vc-x1.

OK to commit and push this one?

message.content: Hmm, the chore title is "docs: zero-copy ring buffer design" no "Pick up" also change "pick up todo," in -0 to "update todo," and since each step will have the same title let's append the sub-step as "(ss-0)" so each will be unique. Thoughts.

content.text:
Good catches on both — the ladder `-0` line should say "update todo," and I'll fix that. On the shared-title-plus-`(ss-N)` idea, here are my thoughts:

**What the protocol currently expects:** each step gets its *own* conventional-commit title (e.g. `docs: ring buffer requirements + constraints` for `-1`), and only the **close-out** commit's title matches the chores header verbatim. The identical-title rule ties one step's three records together (its ladder line, its chores header, its commit) — it doesn't ask all steps in a cycle to share a title. So uniqueness is already solved: distinct titles per step, and the version-of-record (`0.3.0-N` in version.toml) pins each commit's step.

**Your proposal** (all steps titled `docs: zero-copy ring buffer design (ss-0)`, `(ss-1)`, …) works and has a real benefit — `git log --grep "zero-copy ring buffer design"` returns the whole cycle. Two costs:

- It rubs against the "titles carry no trailing suffix" rule. That rule's stated rationale (versions unstable pre-merge) barely applies to sub-steps, but a `(ss-N)` is the same shape and has the same fragility: insert or reorder a step and the numbering shifts, requiring rewrites of already-committed titles or tolerating drift.
- Uniform titles say less in `jj log` — you can't see what a commit did without opening it.

**My recommendation:** keep distinct per-step titles (protocol default), all sharing a common greppable stem, e.g. `docs: ring buffer requirements`, `docs: ring buffer API + layout design`, `feat: ring buffer prototype crate` — "ring buffer" grep still collects the cycle, titles stay informative, no numbering to maintain. But if you prefer the uniform `(ss-N)` scheme, it's workable — I'd then record the convention in AGENTS.md so future cycles follow it consistently.

Which way do you want to go?

message.content: Let's go with your recommendation, distinct per-step titles. But let's fix  AGENTS/cycle-protocol now too.

content.text: I'll make the edits: the ladder wording fix, plus the per-step-title clarification in AGENTS.md and cycle-protocol.md.

**Title:** `docs: pick up zero-copy ring buffer design`

**Body:**
```
Open the 0.3.0 cycle: Todo #1 moves to In Progress with a
four-step ladder (requirements, design doc, prototype,
close-out). Also record the distinct-per-step commit-title
convention this cycle surfaced.

- notes/todo.md: move Todo #1 into In Progress with ladder
- notes/chores/chores-01.md: open 'docs: zero-copy ring
  buffer design' section with empty Commits: line
- version.toml: bump to 0.3.0-0
- AGENTS.md: clarify title identity is per step, not per
  cycle; recommend a shared greppable stem
- notes/cycle-protocol.md: add 'Distinct per step' bullet
  to the Title rules, linking to AGENTS.md
```

The doc changes say: each step gets its own descriptive title (no shared cycle title with a step marker), the chores header carries the close-out title, and step titles share a greppable stem so `git log --grep` collects the cycle.

message.content: Before committing above you said "1. Title is 42 chars, fine — but note the chores header stays docs: zero-copy ring buffer design (the anticipated close-out title), which is correct per protocol." We're now changing the "protocol" so we need to modify that statment, do you agree?

content.text:
Partly agree, mostly not — let me separate the two threads:

**The chores-header statement itself still holds.** The change we just made doesn't alter that rule — it *reaffirms* it. The new AGENTS.md text says explicitly: "The cycle's chores section header carries the anticipated *close-out* title." So `## docs: zero-copy ring buffer design` staying as the chores header while the `-0` commit is titled `docs: pick up zero-copy ring buffer design` is exactly what the updated protocol prescribes. What we changed was the misreading the header invited — that all steps share that title — not the header rule. (And the `-0` title complies with the new stem rule: it contains "ring buffer".)

**But your instinct that something is inconsistent is right — it's the ladder lines.** The Conventional-commit shape rule says a step's ladder entry, chores header, and commit title line up *verbatim* per step. Our ladder currently has informal descriptions ("update todo, open chores, bump version"), not the commit titles. Strictly applied, the ladder should read:

```
- 0.3.0-0 docs: pick up zero-copy ring buffer design (current)
- 0.3.0-1 docs: ring buffer requirements + constraints
- 0.3.0-2 docs: ring buffer API + memory-layout design
- 0.3.0-3 feat: ring buffer prototype crate
- 0.3.0 docs: zero-copy ring buffer design
```

with the later titles being anticipated (provisional until each step commits, synced as the last edit before its push — the protocol already allows that).

Want me to sync the ladder to that form before we commit `-0`?

message.content: Yes, sync the ladder, then commit and push

input.content:
# Ring buffer design

Design notes for the zero-copy ring buffer. This file uses
[Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). This step covers
requirements and constraints; the API and memory-layout design
follows in the next cycle step.

## Goal

A ring buffer for passing typed messages between a producer and
a consumer with no copying at the boundary: the producer writes
its message directly into buffer memory, the consumer reads it
in place. The same layout must work intra-process (threads) and
inter-process (shared memory).

## Requirements

- **no_std** — the crate builds with `#![no_std]` and no
  `alloc`: fixed capacity, operating over a caller-provided
  memory region. Suitable for embedded and kernel-adjacent use.
- **Zero-copy via zerocopy** — messages are structs implementing
  the [zerocopy](https://docs.rs/zerocopy) traits
  (`IntoBytes`, `FromBytes`, `KnownLayout`, `Immutable`); the
  buffer transmutes slots into and out of `&T`/`&mut T` with no
  serialization step and no `unsafe` at the call site.
- **IPC-suitable layout** — the control block (head/tail
  indices) and the slot array live in one contiguous region that
  can be mapped into multiple address spaces:
  - `#[repr(C)]` explicit layout, stable across processes
    compiled from the same source.
  - No pointers inside the region — offsets and indices only.
- **SPSC first** — single producer, single consumer, lock-free
  via atomic head/tail. MPMC is out of scope for this cycle;
  the layout should not preclude adding it later.
- **In-place access API** — reserve/commit on the producer side
  and peek/release on the consumer side, exposing references
  into the buffer rather than returning values by move.
- **Safety** — all `unsafe` encapsulated inside the crate with
  documented invariants; public API is safe.

## Constraints

- **Capacity is a power of two** — index wrapping is a mask,
  no division; checked at construction.
- **Monotonic indices** — head/tail are free-running unsigned
  counters (wrap via masking), so full/empty are
  distinguishable without a separate flag or a sacrificial
  slot.
- **Cache-line separation** — producer- and consumer-owned
  indices are padded/aligned to separate cache lines to avoid
  false sharing.
- **Fixed-size slots** — this design is `RingBuffer<T>` with
  one `T` per slot. Variable-length framed messages are a
  possible follow-on, not in this cycle.
- **zerocopy, core-only** — depend on zerocopy with default
  features compatible with `no_std`; no other runtime
  dependencies.

## Open questions

- Atomic width for indices: `AtomicU32` vs `AtomicUsize` —
  cross-process mapping between 32/64-bit peers argues for a
  fixed-width `AtomicU32`. Decide in the API/layout step.
- Whether the consumer releases slots one-at-a-time or in
  batches (batch release amortizes the atomic store).
- How construction over foreign memory (an mmap'd region) is
  expressed safely — likely a `from_bytes`-style validated
  cast via zerocopy's `KnownLayout`.

content.text:
Work for `0.3.0-1` is complete. Please review. What changed:

- **`notes/ring-buffer-design.md`** (new) — requirements + constraints for the ring buffer:
  - *Requirements:* no_std/no alloc over caller-provided memory; zero-copy in/out via zerocopy traits (`IntoBytes`/`FromBytes`/`KnownLayout`/`Immutable`); IPC-suitable `#[repr(C)]` layout with no internal pointers; SPSC lock-free first (MPMC not precluded); in-place reserve/commit + peek/release API; all `unsafe` encapsulated.
  - *Constraints:* power-of-two capacity (mask wrapping), free-running monotonic indices (no sacrificial slot), cache-line-separated head/tail, fixed-size `RingBuffer<T>` slots (variable-length deferred), zerocopy as the only dependency.
  - *Open questions* for the `-2` layout step: `AtomicU32` vs `AtomicUsize` (cross-process 32/64-bit argues fixed-width), batch vs single-slot release, and safe construction over an mmap'd region.
- **Bookkeeping:** ladder `-1` marked `(current)`, version bumped to `0.3.0-1`, `Commits:` backfilled in chores-01 for the two commits the last pushes made permanent (`[[1]]` template cleanup, `[[2]]` the `-0` pickup), and a design-notes link added to the In Progress block.

message.content: In Constraints you say "Fixed-size slots" define slot, from my PoV a ring buffer holds M buffers of N size. M is power-of-two, N is modulo cache line size and appropriately aligned. Agreed?

content.text:
Agreed, with one nuance worth writing down. Your model — M slots of N bytes, M a power of two, N a multiple of the cache line and cache-line aligned — is cleaner than what I wrote, because it decouples the slot geometry from the message type: the slot is a property of the buffer; `T` is a view into a slot, valid when `size_of::<T>() <= N` and `align_of::<T>()` divides the slot alignment. Cache-line-sized/aligned slots also kill producer/consumer false sharing on adjacent slots and give every `T` (align ≤ 64) a home for free.

The nuance: N being a cache-line multiple trades space for isolation — a 16-byte message burns 64 bytes. For an IPC-oriented design that's the right default; noting it as a deliberate trade keeps a future "packed slots" variant honest. Updating the doc:

input.old_string:
- **Capacity is a power of two** — index wrapping is a mask,
  no division; checked at construction.
input.new_string:
- **Slot geometry: M slots × N bytes** — a *slot* is one
  fixed-size buffer in the ring; the ring holds M of them.

# Ring buffer design

Design notes for the zero-copy ring buffer. This file uses
[Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). This step covers
requirements and constraints; the API and memory-layout design
follows in the next cycle step.

## Goal

A ring buffer for passing typed messages between a producer and
a consumer with no copying at the boundary: the producer writes
its message directly into buffer memory, the consumer reads it
in place. The same layout must work intra-process (threads) and
inter-process (shared memory).

## Requirements

- **no_std** — the crate builds with `#![no_std]` and no
  `alloc`: fixed capacity, operating over a caller-provided
  memory region. Suitable for embedded and kernel-adjacent use.
- **Zero-copy via zerocopy** — messages are structs implementing
  the [zerocopy](https://docs.rs/zerocopy) traits
  (`IntoBytes`, `FromBytes`, `KnownLayout`, `Immutable`); the
  buffer transmutes slots into and out of `&T`/`&mut T` with no
  serialization step and no `unsafe` at the call site.
- **IPC-suitable layout** — the control block (head/tail
  indices) and the slot array live in one contiguous region that
  can be mapped into multiple address spaces:
  - `#[repr(C)]` explicit layout, stable across processes
    compiled from the same source.
  - No pointers inside the region — offsets and indices only.
- **SPSC first** — single producer, single consumer, lock-free
  via atomic head/tail. MPMC is out of scope for this cycle;
  the layout should not preclude adding it later.
- **In-place access API** — reserve/commit on the producer side
  and peek/release on the consumer side, exposing references
  into the buffer rather than returning values by move.
- **Safety** — all `unsafe` encapsulated inside the crate with
  documented invariants; public API is safe.

## Constraints

- **Capacity is a power of two** — index wrapping is a mask,
  no division; checked at construction.
- **Monotonic indices** — head/tail are free-running unsigned
  counters (wrap via masking), so full/empty are
  distinguishable without a separate flag or a sacrificial
  slot.
- **Cache-line separation** — producer- and consumer-owned
  indices are padded/aligned to separate cache lines to avoid
  false sharing.
- **Fixed-size slots** — this design is `RingBuffer<T>` with
  one `T` per slot. Variable-length framed messages are a
  possible follow-on, not in this cycle.
- **zerocopy, core-only** — depend on zerocopy with default
  features compatible with `no_std`; no other runtime
  dependencies.

## Open questions

- Atomic width for indices: `AtomicU32` vs `AtomicUsize` —
  cross-process mapping between 32/64-bit peers argues for a
  fixed-width `AtomicU32`. Decide in the API/layout step.
- Whether the consumer releases slots one-at-a-time or in
  batches (batch release amortizes the atomic store).
- How construction over foreign memory (an mmap'd region) is
  expressed safely — likely a `from_bytes`-style validated
  cast via zerocopy's `KnownLayout`.

[Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). This step covers
requirements and constraints; the API and memory-layout design
follows in the next cycle step.

## Goal

a consumer with no copying at the boundary: the producer writes
its message directly into buffer memory, the consumer reads it

## Requirements

- **no_std** — the crate builds with `#![no_std]` and no
  `alloc`: fixed capacity, operating over a caller-provided

- **Zero-copy via zerocopy** — messages are structs implementing
  the [zerocopy](https://docs.rs/zerocopy) traits
  (`IntoBytes`, `FromBytes`, `KnownLayout`, `Immutable`); the
  buffer transmutes slots into and out of `&T`/`&mut T` with no
  serialization step and no `unsafe` at the call site.
- **IPC-suitable layout** — the control block (head/tail
  indices) and the slot array live in one contiguous region that
  can be mapped into multiple address spaces:
  - `#[repr(C)]` explicit layout, stable across processes
    compiled from the same source.
  - No pointers inside the region — offsets and indices only.
- **SPSC first** — single producer, single consumer, lock-free
  via atomic head/tail. MPMC is out of scope for this cycle;
  the layout should not preclude adding it later.
- **In-place access API** — reserve/commit on the producer side
  and peek/release on the consumer side, exposing references
  into the buffer rather than returning values by move.
- **Safety** — all `unsafe` encapsulated inside the crate with
  documented invariants; public API is safe.

## Constraints

- **Slot geometry: M slots × N bytes** — a *slot* is one
  fixed-size buffer in the ring; the ring holds M of them.
  - M (capacity) is a power of two — index wrapping is a
    mask, no division; checked at construction.
  - N (slot size) is a multiple of the cache-line size, and
    each slot is cache-line aligned — adjacent slots never
    share a line, and any `T` with `align_of::<T>()` up to
    the line size fits without padding logic.

  distinguishable without a separate flag or a sacrificial
  slot.
- **Cache-line separation** — producer- and consumer-owned
  indices are padded/aligned to separate cache lines to avoid
  false sharing.
- **Fixed-size slots** — this design is `RingBuffer<T>` with
  one `T` per slot. Variable-length framed messages are a
  possible follow-on, not in this cycle.
- **zerocopy, core-only** — depend on zerocopy with default
  features compatible with `no_std`; no other runtime
  dependencies.

## Open questions

- Atomic width for indices: `AtomicU32` vs `AtomicUsize` —
  cross-process mapping between 32/64-bit peers argues for a
  fixed-width `AtomicU32`. Decide in the API/layout step.
- Whether the consumer releases slots one-at-a-time or in
  batches (batch release amortizes the atomic store).
- How construction over foreign memory (an mmap'd region) is
  expressed safely — likely a `from_bytes`-style validated
  cast via zerocopy's `KnownLayout`.

lastPrompt: In Constraints you say "Fixed-size slots" define slot, from my PoV a ring buffer holds M buffers of N size. M is power-of-two, N is modulo cache line size and appropriately aligned. Agreed?

content.text:
Done. The Constraints section now defines the slot explicitly:

- **Slot geometry: M slots × N bytes** — slot = one fixed-size buffer in the ring; M power-of-two (mask wrapping), N a cache-line multiple with each slot cache-line aligned; the space-for-isolation trade is noted with a packed-slot variant as possible follow-on.
- The old "Fixed-size slots" bullet became **Typed access over untyped slots** — geometry belongs to the buffer, `T` is a zerocopy view valid when `size_of::<T>() <= N` and its alignment divides the slot alignment.

The separate "Cache-line separation" bullet (head/tail indices on their own lines) still stands — that one is about the control block, not slots. Anything else in the doc, or ready to review for commit?

This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
contains near term tasks with a short description and
uses links or reference links for more details.

## In Progress

**docs: zero-copy ring buffer design**

Design a zero-copy ring buffer in no_std Rust using the
zerocopy crate to transmute structs into and out of the
buffer, suitable for inter- and intra-process communication.
Design notes: [ring-buffer-design.md](ring-buffer-design.md).

Plan:
- 0.3.0-0 docs: pick up zero-copy ring buffer design (done)
- 0.3.0-1 docs: ring buffer requirements + constraints (current)
- 0.3.0-2 docs: ring buffer API + memory-layout design
- 0.3.0-3 feat: ring buffer prototype crate
- 0.3.0 docs: zero-copy ring buffer design

## Todo

 Entries are in **strict priority rank** — #1 highest,
 descending. Reprioritize by moving an entry, then
 `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run notes/todo.md` to renumber.
 The numbers are positional rank, not stable IDs — to refer
 to a Todo, name it by its **title** (a greppable mention;
 a numbered list item has no anchor to link to), not its
 number. Long-tail entries
 live in [todo-backlog.md](todo-backlog.md). Use the
 [Prose Form in AGENTS.md](../AGENTS.md#prose-form); deeper
 detail goes in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` design
 subsections (link via `[N]` ref).


## Done

and older `## Done` sections are moved to [done.md](done.md) to keep this file small.

# Chores-01

contain discussions and notes on various chores in github compatible
markdown. There is also a [todo.md](../todo.md) file that tracks
tasks and in general there should be a chore section for each task
with the why and how this task will be completed.

## docs: completed dummy chore (0.1.0)

A completed dummy chore description.

## chore: dummy chore (TBD)

A dummy chore description.

## docs: versioning SSOT + generic conventions

Commits:

Adopt the converged generic convention set from fc, so the
template's `AGENTS.md`, `cycle-protocol.md`, and `versioning.md`
are byte-identical to fc's — future repos copy them verbatim and
edit nothing. The template gains the versioning SSOT and the
push-approval carve-out.

- New `notes/versioning.md`: the versioning single source of
  truth. Its `Recording the version-of-record` section states
  Manifest / Notation / Reporter / Cadence as
  conditionals-by-medium, so the file is fully generic — no
  per-project block.
- `cycle-protocol.md` gains the Commits-backfill machinery and
  the "explicit scoped delegation waives the gates" push-approval
  carve-out; `Numbering` defers to versioning.md.
- `AGENTS.md` absorbs fc's generic blocks (Committing-vs-pushing,
  one-command-per-invocation, Use-jj, Conventional-commit shape)
  and drops the `(<version>)` title suffix.

project rather than a template.

  gains a one-line project intro (zero-copy ring buffer
  experiment 1).
- `notes/todo.md` drops the dummy template entries and their
  references; the ring-buffer design task becomes Todo #1.

## Open questions

  cross-process mapping between 32/64-bit peers argues for a
  fixed-width `AtomicU32`. Decide in the API/layout step.
- Whether the consumer releases slots one-at-a-time or in

input.new_string:
## Memory layout

One contiguous `#[repr(C)]` region, mappable into multiple
address spaces. Three parts, each cache-line aligned:

```
offset 0                 Header        (1 cache line)
offset 64                producer line (1 cache line)
offset 128               consumer line (1 cache line)
offset 192               slots: M × N bytes
```

- **Header** (`#[repr(C, align(64))]`) — immutable after init:
  - `magic: u32` — layout marker, rejects foreign regions.
  - `layout_version: u32` — bumped on any layout change.
  - `slot_size: u32` — N in bytes, cache-line multiple.
  - `capacity: u32` — M, power of two; index mask is `M - 1`.
- **Producer line** — `head: AtomicU32` + padding. Written
  only by the producer, read by the consumer.
- **Consumer line** — `tail: AtomicU32` + padding. Written
  only by the consumer, read by the producer.
- **Slots** — `M` slots of `N` bytes, each cache-line
  aligned (N is a line multiple, so alignment follows from
  the array base).

Index scheme (resolves the atomic-width question):

- Indices are **fixed-width `AtomicU32`**, free-running
  (never masked in storage) — fixed width keeps the layout
  identical for 32- and 64-bit peers sharing the region.
- Slot index = `counter & (M - 1)`; occupancy =
  `head.wrapping_sub(tail)` — valid while `M <= 2^31`.
- Empty: `head == tail`. Full: `head - tail == M`. No
  sacrificial slot.

Ordering: producer loads `tail` with `Acquire` and publishes
`head` with `Release`; consumer mirrors (loads `head`
`Acquire`, publishes `tail` `Release`).

## API sketch

Construction (resolves the foreign-memory question):

- `Ring::init(region: &mut [u8], slot_size: u32, capacity:
  u32) -> Result<&Ring, Error>` — writes the header, zeroes
  indices; validates geometry (power-of-two M, line-multiple
  N, region big enough, alignment).
- `Ring::attach(region: &[u8]) -> Result<&Ring, Error>` —
  validated cast of an already-initialized region (an mmap'd
  segment): checks magic, layout_version, geometry against
  the region length. Built on zerocopy's `KnownLayout`
  validated reference casts — no `unsafe` at the call site.
- `ring.split() -> (Producer<'_>, Consumer<'_>)` — the two
  endpoint handles; each is `Send + !Sync` and exists at
  most once per ring per process (SPSC discipline;
  cross-process there is one producer process and one
  consumer process by contract).

Producer — reserve/commit, in place:

- `producer.reserve::<T>() -> Result<Reserved<'_, T>, Full>`
  — next free slot as `&mut T` (zerocopy `FromBytes +
  IntoBytes + KnownLayout`); `T` geometry checked against
  `slot_size` once.
- `Reserved::commit(self)` — publishes the slot
  (`head + 1`, `Release`). Dropping without commit abandons
  the reservation (nothing published).

Consumer — peek/release, in place:

- `consumer.peek::<T>() -> Result<Peeked<'_, T>, Empty>` —
  oldest unread slot as `&T`.
- `Peeked::release(self)` — frees the slot (`tail + 1`,
  `Release`). Dropping without release leaves the slot
  unread — the next peek returns it again.

Batch release (resolves the batching question): deferred —
the guard API leaves room for a `release_n(n)` later; the
single-slot guard is the whole surface this cycle.

## Open questions

- Cross-process trust: `attach` validates the header, but a
  hostile peer can scribble on the region afterward; the
  consumer-side typed view means a torn/garbage message is
  still *some* valid `T` (zerocopy guarantees no UB, not
  semantic sense). Document as: the ring is safe against UB,
  not against a malicious peer.
- Whether `split()` needs a runtime once-guard in the header
  (endpoint-claimed flags) or stays a documented contract.

# Ring buffer design

Design notes for the zero-copy ring buffer. This file uses
[Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). This step covers
requirements and constraints; the API and memory-layout design
follows in the next cycle step.

## Goal

A ring buffer for passing typed messages between a producer and
a consumer with no copying at the boundary: the producer writes
its message directly into buffer memory, the consumer reads it
in place. The same layout must work intra-process (threads) and
inter-process (shared memory).

## Requirements

- **no_std** — the crate builds with `#![no_std]` and no
  `alloc`: fixed capacity, operating over a caller-provided
  memory region. Suitable for embedded and kernel-adjacent use.
- **Zero-copy via zerocopy** — messages are structs implementing
  the [zerocopy](https://docs.rs/zerocopy) traits
  (`IntoBytes`, `FromBytes`, `KnownLayout`, `Immutable`); the
  buffer transmutes slots into and out of `&T`/`&mut T` with no
  serialization step and no `unsafe` at the call site.
- **IPC-suitable layout** — the control block (head/tail
  indices) and the slot array live in one contiguous region that
  can be mapped into multiple address spaces:
  - `#[repr(C)]` explicit layout, stable across processes
    compiled from the same source.
  - No pointers inside the region — offsets and indices only.
- **SPSC first** — single producer, single consumer, lock-free
  via atomic head/tail. MPMC is out of scope for this cycle;
  the layout should not preclude adding it later.
- **In-place access API** — reserve/commit on the producer side
  and peek/release on the consumer side, exposing references
  into the buffer rather than returning values by move.
- **Safety** — all `unsafe` encapsulated inside the crate with
  documented invariants; public API is safe.

## Constraints

- **Slot geometry: M slots × N bytes** — a *slot* is one
  fixed-size buffer in the ring; the ring holds M of them.
  - M (capacity) is a power of two — index wrapping is a
    mask, no division; checked at construction.
  - N (slot size) is a multiple of the cache-line size, and
    each slot is cache-line aligned — adjacent slots never
    share a line, and any `T` with `align_of::<T>()` up to
    the line size fits without padding logic.
  - This trades space for isolation (a 16-byte message
    occupies a full 64-byte slot) — the right default for
    IPC; a packed-slot variant is a possible follow-on.
- **Monotonic indices** — head/tail are free-running unsigned
  counters (wrap via masking), so full/empty are
  distinguishable without a separate flag or a sacrificial
  slot.
- **Cache-line separation** — producer- and consumer-owned
  indices are padded/aligned to separate cache lines to avoid
  false sharing.
- **Typed access over untyped slots** — the slot geometry
  (M, N) is a property of the buffer, independent of any
  message type; a `T` is a zerocopy view into a slot, valid
  when `size_of::<T>() <= N` and `align_of::<T>()` divides
  the slot alignment (checked at construction or compile
  time). Variable-length framed messages are a possible
  follow-on, not in this cycle.
- **zerocopy, core-only** — depend on zerocopy with default
  features compatible with `no_std`; no other runtime
  dependencies.

## Open questions

- Atomic width for indices: `AtomicU32` vs `AtomicUsize` —
  cross-process mapping between 32/64-bit peers argues for a
  fixed-width `AtomicU32`. Decide in the API/layout step.
- Whether the consumer releases slots one-at-a-time or in
  batches (batch release amortizes the atomic store).
- How construction over foreign memory (an mmap'd region) is
  expressed safely — likely a `from_bytes`-style validated
  cast via zerocopy's `KnownLayout`.

```
offset 0                 Header        (1 cache line)
offset 64                producer line (1 cache line)
offset 128               consumer line (1 cache line)
offset 192               slots: M × N bytes

- **Consumer line** — `tail: AtomicU32` + padding. Written

content.text:
Work for `0.3.0-2` is complete. Please review. What the design adds to `notes/ring-buffer-design.md`:

**Memory layout** — one contiguous `#[repr(C)]` region: a header line (magic, layout_version, slot_size N, capacity M), a producer-owned cache line (`head: AtomicU32`), a consumer-owned cache line (`tail: AtomicU32`), then M×N slots at offset 192. Indices are free-running fixed-width `AtomicU32` (resolves the atomic-width question — same layout for 32/64-bit peers); slot index is `counter & (M-1)`, empty is `head == tail`, full is `head - tail == M`, no sacrificial slot. Acquire/Release pairing on the opposing index.

**API sketch** —
- `Ring::init(region, slot_size, capacity)` and `Ring::attach(region)` (validated cast via zerocopy `KnownLayout` — resolves the foreign-memory question), then `ring.split()` → `Producer` + `Consumer` handles.
- Producer: `reserve::<T>()` → `&mut T` guard; `commit()` publishes, drop abandons.
- Consumer: `peek::<T>()` → `&T` guard; `release()` frees, drop leaves it unread.
- Batch release deferred (guard API leaves room for `release_n`).

**Open questions** (rolled forward): the cross-process trust model (safe against UB, not against a malicious peer scribbling post-attach), and whether `split()` needs endpoint-claimed flags in the header vs a documented contract.

Bookkeeping also done: ladder `-2` `(current)`, version `0.3.0-2`, chores `Commits:` backfilled with `[[3]]` (the `-1` SHA).

content.text:
I'm on board with "one struct" — but I'd push back on the flat field list, on two grounds:

1. **False sharing.** With `producer_idx` and `consumer_idx` adjacent, every producer store invalidates the consumer's cached line and vice versa — the exact ping-pong the `-1` constraint ("cache-line separation") was written to avoid. On a shared-memory SPSC ring this is the dominant cost; it's why the current layout gives each index its own line.
2. **Atomicity.** The indices are concurrently written/read across threads/processes, so they must be `AtomicU32`, not plain `u32` — plain fields would be a data race (UB), and zerocopy-casting a shared region to a struct with non-atomic mutable fields doesn't make it sound.

The compromise that keeps your shape and the performance constraint: **one `Header` struct containing all six fields, with the two indices padded onto their own cache lines**:

```rust
#[repr(C, align(64))]
struct Header {
    // line 0 — immutable after init
    magic: u32,
    layout_version: u32,
    slot_size: u32,
    capacity: u32,
    _pad0: [u8; 48],

    producer_idx: AtomicU32,
    _pad1: [u8; 60],
    // line 2 — consumer-owned
    consumer_idx: AtomicU32,
    _pad2: [u8; 60],
}
```

I'd also adopt your field names `producer_idx`/`consumer_idx` over `head`/`tail` — self-describing and unambiguous about who owns which.

Want me to update the design doc to this single-struct form (three-line `Header`, your names, indices atomic)?

# Ring buffer design

Design notes for the zero-copy ring buffer. This file uses
[Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It covers requirements,
constraints, the memory layout, and the API sketch; the
prototype crate validates it in the next cycle step.

## Goal

A ring buffer for passing typed messages between a producer and
a consumer with no copying at the boundary: the producer writes
its message directly into buffer memory, the consumer reads it
in place. The same layout must work intra-process (threads) and
inter-process (shared memory).

## Requirements

- **no_std** — the crate builds with `#![no_std]` and no
  `alloc`: fixed capacity, operating over a caller-provided
  memory region. Suitable for embedded and kernel-adjacent use.
- **Zero-copy via zerocopy** — messages are structs implementing
  the [zerocopy](https://docs.rs/zerocopy) traits
  (`IntoBytes`, `FromBytes`, `KnownLayout`, `Immutable`); the
  buffer transmutes slots into and out of `&T`/`&mut T` with no
  serialization step and no `unsafe` at the call site.
- **IPC-suitable layout** — the control block (head/tail
  indices) and the slot array live in one contiguous region that
  can be mapped into multiple address spaces:
  - `#[repr(C)]` explicit layout, stable across processes
    compiled from the same source.
  - No pointers inside the region — offsets and indices only.
- **SPSC first** — single producer, single consumer, lock-free
  via atomic head/tail. MPMC is out of scope for this cycle;
  the layout should not preclude adding it later.
- **In-place access API** — reserve/commit on the producer side
  and peek/release on the consumer side, exposing references
  into the buffer rather than returning values by move.
- **Safety** — all `unsafe` encapsulated inside the crate with
  documented invariants; public API is safe.

## Constraints

- **Slot geometry: M slots × N bytes** — a *slot* is one
  fixed-size buffer in the ring; the ring holds M of them.
  - M (capacity) is a power of two — index wrapping is a
    mask, no division; checked at construction.
  - N (slot size) is a multiple of the cache-line size, and
    each slot is cache-line aligned — adjacent slots never
    share a line, and any `T` with `align_of::<T>()` up to
    the line size fits without padding logic.
  - This trades space for isolation (a 16-byte message
    occupies a full 64-byte slot) — the right default for
    IPC; a packed-slot variant is a possible follow-on.
- **Monotonic indices** — head/tail are free-running unsigned
  counters (wrap via masking), so full/empty are
  distinguishable without a separate flag or a sacrificial
  slot.
- **Cache-line separation** — producer- and consumer-owned
  indices are padded/aligned to separate cache lines to avoid
  false sharing.
- **Typed access over untyped slots** — the slot geometry
  (M, N) is a property of the buffer, independent of any
  message type; a `T` is a zerocopy view into a slot, valid
  when `size_of::<T>() <= N` and `align_of::<T>()` divides
  the slot alignment (checked at construction or compile
  time). Variable-length framed messages are a possible
  follow-on, not in this cycle.
- **zerocopy, core-only** — depend on zerocopy with default
  features compatible with `no_std`; no other runtime
  dependencies.

## Memory layout

One contiguous `#[repr(C)]` region, mappable into multiple
address spaces. Three parts, each cache-line aligned:

```
offset 0                 Header        (1 cache line)
offset 64                producer line (1 cache line)
offset 128               consumer line (1 cache line)
offset 192               slots: M × N bytes
```

- **Header** (`#[repr(C, align(64))]`) — immutable after init:
  - `magic: u32` — layout marker, rejects foreign regions.
  - `layout_version: u32` — bumped on any layout change.
  - `slot_size: u32` — N in bytes, cache-line multiple.
  - `capacity: u32` — M, power of two; index mask is `M - 1`.
- **Producer line** — `head: AtomicU32` + padding. Written
  only by the producer, read by the consumer.
- **Consumer line** — `tail: AtomicU32` + padding. Written
  only by the consumer, read by the producer.
- **Slots** — `M` slots of `N` bytes, each cache-line
  aligned (N is a line multiple, so alignment follows from
  the array base).

Index scheme (resolves the atomic-width question):

- Indices are **fixed-width `AtomicU32`**, free-running
  (never masked in storage) — fixed width keeps the layout
  identical for 32- and 64-bit peers sharing the region.
- Slot index = `counter & (M - 1)`; occupancy =
  `head.wrapping_sub(tail)` — valid while `M <= 2^31`.
- Empty: `head == tail`. Full: `head - tail == M`. No
  sacrificial slot.

Ordering: producer loads `tail` with `Acquire` and publishes
`head` with `Release`; consumer mirrors (loads `head`
`Acquire`, publishes `tail` `Release`).

## API sketch

Construction (resolves the foreign-memory question):

- `Ring::init(region: &mut [u8], slot_size: u32, capacity:
  u32) -> Result<&Ring, Error>` — writes the header, zeroes
  indices; validates geometry (power-of-two M, line-multiple
  N, region big enough, alignment).
- `Ring::attach(region: &[u8]) -> Result<&Ring, Error>` —
  validated cast of an already-initialized region (an mmap'd
  segment): checks magic, layout_version, geometry against
  the region length. Built on zerocopy's `KnownLayout`
  validated reference casts — no `unsafe` at the call site.
- `ring.split() -> (Producer<'_>, Consumer<'_>)` — the two
  endpoint handles; each is `Send + !Sync` and exists at
  most once per ring per process (SPSC discipline;
  cross-process there is one producer process and one
  consumer process by contract).

Producer — reserve/commit, in place:

- `producer.reserve::<T>() -> Result<Reserved<'_, T>, Full>`
  — next free slot as `&mut T` (zerocopy `FromBytes +
  IntoBytes + KnownLayout`); `T` geometry checked against
  `slot_size` once.
- `Reserved::commit(self)` — publishes the slot
  (`head + 1`, `Release`). Dropping without commit abandons
  the reservation (nothing published).

Consumer — peek/release, in place:

- `consumer.peek::<T>() -> Result<Peeked<'_, T>, Empty>` —
  oldest unread slot as `&T`.
- `Peeked::release(self)` — frees the slot (`tail + 1`,
  `Release`). Dropping without release leaves the slot
  unread — the next peek returns it again.

Batch release (resolves the batching question): deferred —
the guard API leaves room for a `release_n(n)` later; the
single-slot guard is the whole surface this cycle.

## Open questions

- Cross-process trust: `attach` validates the header, but a
  hostile peer can scribble on the region afterward; the
  consumer-side typed view means a torn/garbage message is
  still *some* valid `T` (zerocopy guarantees no UB, not
  semantic sense). Document as: the ring is safe against UB,
  not against a malicious peer.
- Whether `split()` needs a runtime once-guard in the header
  (endpoint-claimed flags) or stays a documented contract.

- **In-place access API** — reserve/commit on the producer side
  and peek/release on the consumer side, exposing references
  into the buffer rather than returning values by move.
- **Safety** — all `unsafe` encapsulated inside the crate with
  documented invariants; public API is safe.

## Constraints

- **Slot geometry: M slots × N bytes** — a *slot* is one
  fixed-size buffer in the ring; the ring holds M of them.
  - M (capacity) is a power of two — index wrapping is a
    mask, no division; checked at construction.
  - N (slot size) is a multiple of the cache-line size, and
    each slot is cache-line aligned — adjacent slots never
    share a line, and any `T` with `align_of::<T>()` up to

    occupies a full 64-byte slot) — the right default for
    IPC; a packed-slot variant is a possible follow-on.

  counters (wrap via masking), so full/empty are
  distinguishable without a separate flag or a sacrificial
  slot.
- **Cache-line separation** — producer- and consumer-owned
  indices are padded/aligned to separate cache lines to avoid
  false sharing.
- **Typed access over untyped slots** — the slot geometry
  (M, N) is a property of the buffer, independent of any
  message type; a `T` is a zerocopy view into a slot, valid
  when `size_of::<T>() <= N` and `align_of::<T>()` divides
  the slot alignment (checked at construction or compile
  time). Variable-length framed messages are a possible
  follow-on, not in this cycle.
- **zerocopy, core-only** — depend on zerocopy with default
  features compatible with `no_std`; no other runtime
  dependencies.

## Memory layout

One contiguous `#[repr(C)]` region, mappable into multiple
address spaces: a `Header` struct followed by the slot array.
The `Header` is one struct spanning three cache lines, so each
concurrently-written index owns its own line:

```rust
#[repr(C, align(64))]

    // line 0 — immutable after init
    magic: u32,          // layout marker, rejects foreign regions
    layout_version: u32, // bumped on any layout change
    slot_size: u32,      // N in bytes, cache-line multiple
    capacity: u32,       // M, power of two; index mask is M - 1
    _pad0: [u8; 48],
    // line 1 — producer-owned
    producer_idx: AtomicU32, // written by producer only
    _pad1: [u8; 60],
    // line 2 — consumer-owned
    consumer_idx: AtomicU32, // written by consumer only
    _pad2: [u8; 60],
}
```

offset 0                    Header (192 bytes, 3 cache lines)
offset size_of::<Header>()  slots: M × N bytes
```

- One type owns the whole control-block layout:
  `size_of::<Header>()` is the slot-array offset, and a
  single zerocopy derive covers validation.
- **Slots** — `M` slots of `N` bytes, each cache-line
  aligned (N is a line multiple, so alignment follows from
  the array base).

Index scheme (resolves the atomic-width question):

- Indices are **fixed-width `AtomicU32`**, free-running
  (never masked in storage) — fixed width keeps the layout
  identical for 32- and 64-bit peers sharing the region.
- Slot index = `counter & (M - 1)`; occupancy =
  `producer_idx.wrapping_sub(consumer_idx)` — valid while
  `M <= 2^31`.
- Empty: `producer_idx == consumer_idx`. Full:
  `producer_idx - consumer_idx == M`. No sacrificial slot.

Ordering: the producer loads `consumer_idx` with `Acquire`
and publishes `producer_idx` with `Release`; the consumer
mirrors (loads `producer_idx` `Acquire`, publishes
`consumer_idx` `Release`).

## API sketch

Construction (resolves the foreign-memory question):

- `Ring::init(region: &mut [u8], slot_size: u32, capacity:
  u32) -> Result<&Ring, Error>` — writes the header, zeroes
  indices; validates geometry (power-of-two M, line-multiple
  N, region big enough, alignment).
- `Ring::attach(region: &[u8]) -> Result<&Ring, Error>` —
  validated cast of an already-initialized region (an mmap'd
  segment): checks magic, layout_version, geometry against
  the region length. Built on zerocopy's `KnownLayout`
  validated reference casts — no `unsafe` at the call site.

  endpoint handles; each is `Send + !Sync` and exists at
  most once per ring per process (SPSC discipline;
  cross-process there is one producer process and one
  consumer process by contract).

  IntoBytes + KnownLayout`); `T` geometry checked against
  `slot_size` once.

  (`head + 1`, `Release`). Dropping without commit abandons
  the reservation (nothing published).

  oldest unread slot as `&T`.

  `Release`). Dropping without release leaves the slot
  unread — the next peek returns it again.

the guard API leaves room for a `release_n(n)` later; the
single-slot guard is the whole surface this cycle.

- Cross-process trust: `attach` validates the header, but a
  hostile peer can scribble on the region afterward; the
  consumer-side typed view means a torn/garbage message is
  still *some* valid `T` (zerocopy guarantees no UB, not
  semantic sense). Document as: the ring is safe against UB,
  not against a malicious peer.

# Ring buffer design

Design notes for the zero-copy ring buffer. This file uses
[Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It covers requirements,
constraints, the memory layout, and the API sketch; the
prototype crate validates it in the next cycle step.

## Goal

A ring buffer for passing typed messages between a producer and
a consumer with no copying at the boundary: the producer writes
its message directly into buffer memory, the consumer reads it
in place. The same layout must work intra-process (threads) and
inter-process (shared memory).

## Requirements

- **no_std** — the crate builds with `#![no_std]` and no
  `alloc`: fixed capacity, operating over a caller-provided
  memory region. Suitable for embedded and kernel-adjacent use.
- **Zero-copy via zerocopy** — messages are structs implementing
  the [zerocopy](https://docs.rs/zerocopy) traits
  (`IntoBytes`, `FromBytes`, `KnownLayout`, `Immutable`); the
  buffer transmutes slots into and out of `&T`/`&mut T` with no
  serialization step and no `unsafe` at the call site.
- **IPC-suitable layout** — the control block (head/tail
  indices) and the slot array live in one contiguous region that
  can be mapped into multiple address spaces:
  - `#[repr(C)]` explicit layout, stable across processes
    compiled from the same source.
  - No pointers inside the region — offsets and indices only.
- **SPSC first** — single producer, single consumer, lock-free
  via atomic head/tail. MPMC is out of scope for this cycle;
  the layout should not preclude adding it later.
- **In-place access API** — reserve/commit on the producer side
  and peek/release on the consumer side, exposing references
  into the buffer rather than returning values by move.
- **Safety** — all `unsafe` encapsulated inside the crate with
  documented invariants; public API is safe.

    each slot is cache-line aligned — adjacent slots never

  fixed-size buffer in the ring; the ring holds M of them.
  - M (capacity) is a power of two — index wrapping is a
    mask, no division; checked at construction.
  - N (slot size) is a multiple of the cache-line size, and
    each slot is cache-line aligned — adjacent slots never
    share a line, and any `T` with `align_of::<T>()` up to
    the line size fits without padding logic.
  - This trades space for isolation (a 16-byte message
    occupies a full 64-byte slot) — the right default for
    IPC; a packed-slot variant is a possible follow-on.
- **Monotonic indices** — producer/consumer indices are
  free-running unsigned counters (wrap via masking), so
  full/empty are distinguishable without a separate flag or
  a sacrificial slot.
- **Cache-line separation** — producer- and consumer-owned
  indices are padded/aligned to separate cache lines to avoid
  false sharing.
- **Typed access over untyped slots** — the slot geometry
  (M, N) is a property of the buffer, independent of any
  message type; a `T` is a zerocopy view into a slot, valid
  when `size_of::<T>() <= N` and `align_of::<T>()` divides
  the slot alignment (checked at construction or compile
  time). Variable-length framed messages are a possible
  follow-on, not in this cycle.
- **zerocopy, core-only** — depend on zerocopy with default
  features compatible with `no_std`; no other runtime
  dependencies.

## Memory layout

One contiguous `#[repr(C)]` region, mappable into multiple
address spaces: a `Header` struct followed by the slot array.
The `Header` is one struct spanning three cache lines, so each
concurrently-written index owns its own line:

```rust
#[repr(C, align(64))]
struct Header {
    // line 0 — immutable after init
    magic: u32,          // layout marker, rejects foreign regions
    layout_version: u32, // bumped on any layout change
    slot_size: u32,      // N in bytes, cache-line multiple
    capacity: u32,       // M, power of two; index mask is M - 1
    _pad0: [u8; 48],
    // line 1 — producer-owned
    producer_idx: AtomicU32, // written by producer only
    _pad1: [u8; 60],
    // line 2 — consumer-owned
    consumer_idx: AtomicU32, // written by consumer only
    _pad2: [u8; 60],
}
```

```
offset 0                    Header (192 bytes, 3 cache lines)
offset size_of::<Header>()  slots: M × N bytes
```

- One type owns the whole control-block layout:
  `size_of::<Header>()` is the slot-array offset, and a
  single zerocopy derive covers validation.
- **Slots** — `M` slots of `N` bytes, each cache-line
  aligned (N is a line multiple, so alignment follows from
  the array base).

Index scheme (resolves the atomic-width question):

- Indices are **fixed-width `AtomicU32`**, free-running
  (never masked in storage) — fixed width keeps the layout
  identical for 32- and 64-bit peers sharing the region.
- Slot index = `counter & (M - 1)`; occupancy =
  `producer_idx.wrapping_sub(consumer_idx)` — valid while
  `M <= 2^31`.
- Empty: `producer_idx == consumer_idx`. Full:
  `producer_idx - consumer_idx == M`. No sacrificial slot.

Ordering: the producer loads `consumer_idx` with `Acquire`
and publishes `producer_idx` with `Release`; the consumer
mirrors (loads `producer_idx` `Acquire`, publishes
`consumer_idx` `Release`).

## API sketch

Construction (resolves the foreign-memory question):

- `Ring::init(region: &mut [u8], slot_size: u32, capacity:
  u32) -> Result<&Ring, Error>` — writes the header, zeroes
  indices; validates geometry (power-of-two M, line-multiple
  N, region big enough, alignment).
- `Ring::attach(region: &[u8]) -> Result<&Ring, Error>` —
  validated cast of an already-initialized region (an mmap'd
  segment): checks magic, layout_version, geometry against
  the region length. Built on zerocopy's `KnownLayout`
  validated reference casts — no `unsafe` at the call site.
- `ring.split() -> (Producer<'_>, Consumer<'_>)` — the two
  endpoint handles; each is `Send + !Sync` and exists at
  most once per ring per process (SPSC discipline;
  cross-process there is one producer process and one
  consumer process by contract).

Producer — reserve/commit, in place:

- `producer.reserve::<T>() -> Result<Reserved<'_, T>, Full>`
  — next free slot as `&mut T` (zerocopy `FromBytes +
  IntoBytes + KnownLayout`); `T` geometry checked against
  `slot_size` once.
- `Reserved::commit(self)` — publishes the slot
  (`producer_idx + 1`, `Release`). Dropping without commit
  abandons the reservation (nothing published).

Consumer — peek/release, in place:

- `consumer.peek::<T>() -> Result<Peeked<'_, T>, Empty>` —
  oldest unread slot as `&T`.
- `Peeked::release(self)` — frees the slot
  (`consumer_idx + 1`, `Release`). Dropping without release
  leaves the slot unread — the next peek returns it again.

Batch release (resolves the batching question): deferred —
the guard API leaves room for a `release_n(n)` later; the
single-slot guard is the whole surface this cycle.

## Open questions

- Cross-process trust: `attach` validates the header, but a
  hostile peer can scribble on the region afterward; the
  consumer-side typed view means a torn/garbage message is
  still *some* valid `T` (zerocopy guarantees no UB, not
  semantic sense). Document as: the ring is safe against UB,
  not against a malicious peer.
- Whether `split()` needs a runtime once-guard in the header
  (endpoint-claimed flags) or stays a documented contract.

# Ring buffer design

Design notes for the zero-copy ring buffer. This file uses
[Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It covers requirements,
constraints, the memory layout, and the API sketch; the
prototype crate validates it in the next cycle step.

## Goal

A ring buffer for passing typed messages between a producer and
a consumer with no copying at the boundary: the producer writes
its message directly into buffer memory, the consumer reads it
in place. The same layout must work intra-process (threads) and
inter-process (shared memory).

## Requirements

- **no_std** — the crate builds with `#![no_std]` and no
  `alloc`: fixed capacity, operating over a caller-provided
  memory region. Suitable for embedded and kernel-adjacent use.
- **Zero-copy via zerocopy** — messages are structs implementing
  the [zerocopy](https://docs.rs/zerocopy) traits
  (`IntoBytes`, `FromBytes`, `KnownLayout`, `Immutable`); the
  buffer transmutes slots into and out of `&T`/`&mut T` with no
  serialization step and no `unsafe` at the call site.
- **IPC-suitable layout** — the control block (the
  producer/consumer indices) and the slot array live in one
  contiguous region that
  can be mapped into multiple address spaces:
  - `#[repr(C)]` explicit layout, stable across processes
    compiled from the same source.
  - No pointers inside the region — offsets and indices only.
- **SPSC first** — single producer, single consumer, lock-free
  via atomic head/tail. MPMC is out of scope for this cycle;
  the layout should not preclude adding it later.
- **In-place access API** — reserve/commit on the producer side
  and peek/release on the consumer side, exposing references
  into the buffer rather than returning values by move.
- **Safety** — all `unsafe` encapsulated inside the crate with
  documented invariants; public API is safe.

  follow-on, not in this cycle.

  features compatible with `no_std`; no other runtime

content.text:
Done. The design now uses a single `Header` struct (three cache lines: immutable geometry line, producer line, consumer line) with `producer_idx`/`consumer_idx: AtomicU32`, and every `head`/`tail` mention throughout the doc is renamed to match — the requirements section, the full/empty math, the ordering rules, and the commit/release guard descriptions. Offsets are unchanged (slots start at `size_of::<Header>()` = 192).

Ready for your review of the `-2` diff — say the word and I'll show the commit description.

message.content: I don't think the math is correct for full, isn't it: `|producer_idx - consuder_idx| + 1 == M`

content.text:
The doc's math is right for the scheme it uses — the confusion is which scheme the indices follow. Let me walk it:

In this design the indices are **free-running counters**, not positions in the array. `producer_idx` counts messages ever committed; `consumer_idx` counts messages ever released. Neither is masked in storage — masking happens only when converting to a slot position (`counter & (M-1)`). So the occupancy (messages written but not yet read) is exactly:

```
occupancy = producer_idx.wrapping_sub(consumer_idx)
```

Concrete example, M = 4: start `p = 0, c = 0` → occupancy 0, empty. Producer commits four messages without the consumer running: `p = 4, c = 0` → occupancy 4 = M, **full** — and correctly so, all four slots hold unread data. Consumer releases one: `c = 1` → occupancy 3, room for one more. So `producer_idx - consumer_idx == M` is the full test, `== 0` is empty, no `+1` anywhere.

The `|p − c| + 1 == M` shape you're recalling comes from the *other* classic scheme, where head and tail are already-masked array positions. There `p == c` is ambiguous (empty or full look identical), so you sacrifice one slot and declare full at `(p + 1) % M == c` — that's where the `+1` lives, and it wastes a slot. Free-running counters are exactly how this design avoids that (the "no sacrificial slot" bullet).

Two details that make it sound: no absolute value — unsigned `wrapping_sub` is correct even after `p` wraps past `u32::MAX` while `c` hasn't (modular arithmetic), whereas `|p − c|` would give a wrong huge value at the wrap boundary; and the `M <= 2^31` constraint keeps occupancy unambiguous in u32 space.

So I'd leave the doc as is — agreed?

lastPrompt: I don't think the math is correct for full, isn't it: `|producer_idx - consuder_idx| + 1 == M`

message.content: So the producer consumer indexes are full u32 values and masked only when used as an index not when incremented?

content.text:
Exactly right. Stored and incremented as full free-running `u32` values — `commit` does `producer_idx.store(p + 1)` (well, wrapping add), never `% M`. The mask is applied only at the moment of slot access: `slot = &slots[(idx & (M - 1)) as usize]`.

Two properties make that work:

- **u32 overflow is harmless** — when `producer_idx` wraps past `u32::MAX` back to 0, `wrapping_sub` still gives correct occupancy, *because* M is a power of two: 2³² is a multiple of M, so the wrap doesn't shift the masked slot position either. (This is the quiet second reason for the power-of-two constraint, beyond cheap masking — with a non-power-of-two M, the u32 wrap would corrupt slot positions.)
- **The invariant `0 <= p − c <= M`** (in wrapping arithmetic) is maintained by each side checking before advancing: producer won't reserve when occupancy = M, consumer won't peek when it's 0.

That wrap subtlety is worth capturing — I'll add a line to the index-scheme bullets in the doc:

input.old_string:
- Empty: `producer_idx == consumer_idx`. Full:
  `producer_idx - consumer_idx == M`. No sacrificial slot.
input.new_string:
- Empty: `producer_idx == consumer_idx`. Full:
  `producer_idx - consumer_idx == M`. No sacrificial slot.
- Increments are wrapping adds, never masked in storage; the
  u32 wrap at `2^32` is harmless *because* M is a power of
  two — `2^32 % M == 0`, so both occupancy math and masked
  slot positions carry across the wrap unchanged.

content.text:
Added. So to your question: yes — full u32 counters, wrapping increments, mask only at slot lookup. The new bullet records why the u32 wrap is safe (2³² is a multiple of any power-of-two M, so occupancy and slot positions both survive the wrap).

Ready to show the `-2` commit description whenever you are.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Ring buffer design

Design notes for the zero-copy ring buffer. This file uses
[Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It covers requirements,
constraints, the memory layout, and the API sketch; the
prototype crate validates it in the next cycle step.

## Goal

A ring buffer for passing typed messages between a producer and
a consumer with no copying at the boundary: the producer writes
its message directly into buffer memory, the consumer reads it
in place. The same layout must work intra-process (threads) and
inter-process (shared memory).

## Requirements

- **no_std** — the crate builds with `#![no_std]` and no
  `alloc`: fixed capacity, operating over a caller-provided
  memory region. Suitable for embedded and kernel-adjacent use.
- **Zero-copy via zerocopy** — messages are structs implementing
  the [zerocopy](https://docs.rs/zerocopy) traits
  (`IntoBytes`, `FromBytes`, `KnownLayout`, `Immutable`); the
  buffer transmutes slots into and out of `&T`/`&mut T` with no
  serialization step and no `unsafe` at the call site.
- **IPC-suitable layout** — the control block (the
  producer/consumer indices) and the slot array live in one
  contiguous region that
  can be mapped into multiple address spaces:
  - `#[repr(C)]` explicit layout, stable across processes
    compiled from the same source.
  - No pointers inside the region — offsets and indices only.
- **SPSC first** — single producer, single consumer, lock-free
  via atomic producer/consumer indices. MPMC is out of scope
  for this cycle;
  the layout should not preclude adding it later.
- **In-place access API** — reserve/commit on the producer side
  and peek/release on the consumer side, exposing references
  into the buffer rather than returning values by move.
- **Safety** — all `unsafe` encapsulated inside the crate with
  documented invariants; public API is safe.

## Constraints

- **Slot geometry: M slots × N bytes** — a *slot* is one
  fixed-size buffer in the ring; the ring holds M of them.
  - M (capacity) is a power of two — index wrapping is a
    mask, no division; checked at construction.
  - N (slot size) is a multiple of the cache-line size, and
    each slot is cache-line aligned — adjacent slots never
    share a line, and any `T` with `align_of::<T>()` up to
    the line size fits without padding logic.
  - This trades space for isolation (a 16-byte message
    occupies a full 64-byte slot) — the right default for
    IPC; a packed-slot variant is a possible follow-on.
- **Monotonic indices** — producer/consumer indices are
  free-running unsigned counters (wrap via masking), so
  full/empty are distinguishable without a separate flag or
  a sacrificial slot.
- **Cache-line separation** — producer- and consumer-owned
  indices are padded/aligned to separate cache lines to avoid
  false sharing.
- **Typed access over untyped slots** — the slot geometry
  (M, N) is a property of the buffer, independent of any
  message type; a `T` is a zerocopy view into a slot, valid
  when `size_of::<T>() <= N` and `align_of::<T>()` divides
  the slot alignment (checked at construction or compile
  time). Variable-length framed messages are a possible
  follow-on, not in this cycle.
- **zerocopy, core-only** — depend on zerocopy with default
  features compatible with `no_std`; no other runtime
  dependencies.

## Memory layout

One contiguous `#[repr(C)]` region, mappable into multiple
address spaces: a `Header` struct followed by the slot array.
The `Header` is one struct spanning three cache lines, so each
concurrently-written index owns its own line:

```rust
#[repr(C, align(64))]
struct Header {
    // line 0 — immutable after init
    magic: u32,          // layout marker, rejects foreign regions
    layout_version: u32, // bumped on any layout change
    slot_size: u32,      // N in bytes, cache-line multiple
    capacity: u32,       // M, power of two; index mask is M - 1
    _pad0: [u8; 48],
    // line 1 — producer-owned
    producer_idx: AtomicU32, // written by producer only
    _pad1: [u8; 60],
    // line 2 — consumer-owned
    consumer_idx: AtomicU32, // written by consumer only
    _pad2: [u8; 60],
}
```

```
offset 0                    Header (192 bytes, 3 cache lines)
offset size_of::<Header>()  slots: M × N bytes
```

- One type owns the whole control-block layout:
  `size_of::<Header>()` is the slot-array offset, and a
  single zerocopy derive covers validation.
- **Slots** — `M` slots of `N` bytes, each cache-line
  aligned (N is a line multiple, so alignment follows from
  the array base).

Index scheme (resolves the atomic-width question):

- Indices are **fixed-width `AtomicU32`**, free-running
  (never masked in storage) — fixed width keeps the layout
  identical for 32- and 64-bit peers sharing the region.
- Slot index = `counter & (M - 1)`; occupancy =
  `producer_idx.wrapping_sub(consumer_idx)` — valid while
  `M <= 2^31`.
- Empty: `producer_idx == consumer_idx`. Full:
  `producer_idx - consumer_idx == M`. No sacrificial slot.
- Increments are wrapping adds, never masked in storage; the
  u32 wrap at `2^32` is harmless *because* M is a power of
  two — `2^32 % M == 0`, so both occupancy math and masked
  slot positions carry across the wrap unchanged.

Ordering: the producer loads `consumer_idx` with `Acquire`
and publishes `producer_idx` with `Release`; the consumer
mirrors (loads `producer_idx` `Acquire`, publishes
`consumer_idx` `Release`).

## API sketch

Construction (resolves the foreign-memory question):

- `Ring::init(region: &mut [u8], slot_size: u32, capacity:
  u32) -> Result<&Ring, Error>` — writes the header, zeroes
  indices; validates geometry (power-of-two M, line-multiple
  N, region big enough, alignment).
- `Ring::attach(region: &[u8]) -> Result<&Ring, Error>` —
  validated cast of an already-initialized region (an mmap'd
  segment): checks magic, layout_version, geometry against
  the region length. Built on zerocopy's `KnownLayout`
  validated reference casts — no `unsafe` at the call site.
- `ring.split() -> (Producer<'_>, Consumer<'_>)` — the two
  endpoint handles; each is `Send + !Sync` and exists at
  most once per ring per process (SPSC discipline;
  cross-process there is one producer process and one
  consumer process by contract).

Producer — reserve/commit, in place:

- `producer.reserve::<T>() -> Result<Reserved<'_, T>, Full>`
  — next free slot as `&mut T` (zerocopy `FromBytes +
  IntoBytes + KnownLayout`); `T` geometry checked against
  `slot_size` once.
- `Reserved::commit(self)` — publishes the slot
  (`producer_idx + 1`, `Release`). Dropping without commit
  abandons the reservation (nothing published).

Consumer — peek/release, in place:

- `consumer.peek::<T>() -> Result<Peeked<'_, T>, Empty>` —
  oldest unread slot as `&T`.
- `Peeked::release(self)` — frees the slot
  (`consumer_idx + 1`, `Release`). Dropping without release
  leaves the slot unread — the next peek returns it again.

Batch release (resolves the batching question): deferred —
the guard API leaves room for a `release_n(n)` later; the
single-slot guard is the whole surface this cycle.

## Open questions

- Cross-process trust: `attach` validates the header, but a
  hostile peer can scribble on the region afterward; the
  consumer-side typed view means a torn/garbage message is
  still *some* valid `T` (zerocopy guarantees no UB, not
  semantic sense). Document as: the ring is safe against UB,
  not against a malicious peer.
- Whether `split()` needs a runtime once-guard in the header
  (endpoint-claimed flags) or stays a documented contract.

-  two — `2^32 % M == 0`, so both occupancy math and masked
-  slot positions carry across the wrap unchanged.
+  - increments are wrapping adds, never masked in storage

# Chores-01

Chores-XX files use [Prose form](../../AGENTS.md#prose-form). They
contain discussions and notes on various chores in github compatible
markdown. There is also a [todo.md](../todo.md) file that tracks
tasks and in general there should be a chore section for each task
with the why and how this task will be completed.

## docs: completed dummy chore (0.1.0)

A completed dummy chore description.

## chore: dummy chore (TBD)

A dummy chore description.

## docs: versioning SSOT + generic conventions

Commits:

Adopt the converged generic convention set from fc, so the
template's `AGENTS.md`, `cycle-protocol.md`, and `versioning.md`
are byte-identical to fc's — future repos copy them verbatim and
edit nothing. The template gains the versioning SSOT and the
push-approval carve-out.

- New `notes/versioning.md`: the versioning single source of
  truth. Its `Recording the version-of-record` section states
  Manifest / Notation / Reporter / Cadence as
  conditionals-by-medium, so the file is fully generic — no
  per-project block.
- `cycle-protocol.md` gains the Commits-backfill machinery and
  the "explicit scoped delegation waives the gates" push-approval
  carve-out; `Numbering` defers to versioning.md.
- `AGENTS.md` absorbs fc's generic blocks (Committing-vs-pushing,
  one-command-per-invocation, Use-jj, Conventional-commit shape)
  and drops the `(<version>)` title suffix.

## docs: template cleanup

Commits: [[1]]

Strip the vc-template-x1 boilerplate so the repo reads as its own
project rather than a template.

- `README.md` loses the template/cloning/assumptions sections and
  gains a one-line project intro (zero-copy ring buffer
  experiment 1).
- `notes/todo.md` drops the dummy template entries and their
  references; the ring-buffer design task becomes Todo #1.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Chores-01

Chores-XX files use [Prose form](../../AGENTS.md#prose-form). They
contain discussions and notes on various chores in github compatible
markdown. There is also a [todo.md](../todo.md) file that tracks
tasks and in general there should be a chore section for each task
with the why and how this task will be completed.

## docs: completed dummy chore (0.1.0)

A completed dummy chore description.

## chore: dummy chore (TBD)

A dummy chore description.

## docs: versioning SSOT + generic conventions

Commits:

Adopt the converged generic convention set from fc, so the
template's `AGENTS.md`, `cycle-protocol.md`, and `versioning.md`
are byte-identical to fc's — future repos copy them verbatim and
edit nothing. The template gains the versioning SSOT and the
push-approval carve-out.

- New `notes/versioning.md`: the versioning single source of
  truth. Its `Recording the version-of-record` section states
  Manifest / Notation / Reporter / Cadence as
  conditionals-by-medium, so the file is fully generic — no
  per-project block.
- `cycle-protocol.md` gains the Commits-backfill machinery and
  the "explicit scoped delegation waives the gates" push-approval
  carve-out; `Numbering` defers to versioning.md.
- `AGENTS.md` absorbs fc's generic blocks (Committing-vs-pushing,
  one-command-per-invocation, Use-jj, Conventional-commit shape)
  and drops the `(<version>)` title suffix.

## docs: template cleanup

Commits: [[1]]

Strip the vc-template-x1 boilerplate so the repo reads as its own
project rather than a template.

- `README.md` loses the template/cloning/assumptions sections and
  gains a one-line project intro (zero-copy ring buffer
  experiment 1).
- `notes/todo.md` drops the dummy template entries and their
  references; the ring-buffer design task becomes Todo #1.

## docs: zero-copy ring buffer design

Commits: [[2]],[[3]],[[4]]

# References

[1]: https://github.com/winksaville/zc-ring-x1/commit/32fec004bd30 "32fec004bd300cc072a052fd0f80882a582c790f"
[2]: https://github.com/winksaville/zc-ring-x1/commit/a559e999f998 "a559e999f998bdeb8a0b0c44643ef1699953beb6"
[3]: https://github.com/winksaville/zc-ring-x1/commit/871506f3066e "871506f3066eaacbeb43244ff4196d4e2f9b1f1c"

name = "zc-ring-x1"
# Version-of-record (see notes/versioning.md): advanced through

# prerelease slot.
version = "0.3.0-3"
edition = "2021"
license = "MIT OR Apache-2.0"
description = "Zero-copy no_std SPSC ring buffer experiment"

[dependencies]
zerocopy = { version = "0.8", default-features = false, features = ["derive"] }

content.text:
Cargo cycle clean: fmt ✓, clippy `-D warnings` ✓, 5/5 tests pass (geometry validation, attach validation, roundtrip with wrap, guard abandonment, and a 100k-message threaded SPSC stress test).

Work for `0.3.0-3` is complete. Please review. What landed:

- **`Cargo.toml`** (new) — `zc-ring-x1` lib crate, edition 2021, sole dependency `zerocopy 0.8` (no default features, derive on). Carries the version-of-record `0.3.0-3` per versioning.md's Rust rule; **`version.toml` is deleted** — the flagged deviation, easy to revert.
- **`src/lib.rs`** (new, ~560 lines) — the design transcribed to code:
  - `Header` exactly as designed: `#[repr(C, align(64))]`, three cache lines, `AtomicU32` indices, with a compile-time `size_of == 192` assert.
  - `Ring::init` (safe, validates geometry/alignment/size) and `Ring::attach` (**unsafe fn** — cross-address-space attach can't be made safe in pure Rust semantics; the safety contract documents the MAP_SHARED-style requirement). `split()` consumes the ring into `Producer` + `Consumer`.
  - `reserve::<T>() → Reserved` (DerefMut, `commit()` publishes Release, drop abandons) and `peek::<T>() → Peeked` (Deref, `release()` frees, drop leaves unread). zerocopy `mut_from_prefix`/`ref_from_prefix` do the typed casts — no unsafe at the message boundary.
  - Free-running indices, mask at slot access, Acquire/Release pairing per the doc.
- **Bookkeeping** — ladder `-3` `(current)`, chores `Commits:` gained `[[4]]` (the `-2` SHA).

One design-doc divergence to note: the doc sketches `attach(region: &[u8])` as safe; the implementation made it `unsafe fn attach(*mut u8, len)`. I'd sync the doc at close-out. Ready for the commit description when you are.

# Todo

This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
contains near term tasks with a short description and
uses links or reference links for more details.

## In Progress

**docs: zero-copy ring buffer design**

Design a zero-copy ring buffer in no_std Rust using the
zerocopy crate to transmute structs into and out of the
buffer, suitable for inter- and intra-process communication.
Design notes: [ring-buffer-design.md](ring-buffer-design.md).

Plan:
- 0.3.0-0 docs: pick up zero-copy ring buffer design (done)
- 0.3.0-1 docs: ring buffer requirements + constraints (done)
- 0.3.0-2 docs: ring buffer API + memory-layout design (done)
- 0.3.0-3 feat: ring buffer prototype crate (current)
- 0.3.0 docs: zero-copy ring buffer design

## Todo

 Entries are in **strict priority rank** — #1 highest,
 descending. Reprioritize by moving an entry, then
 `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run notes/todo.md` to renumber.
 The numbers are positional rank, not stable IDs — to refer
 to a Todo, name it by its **title** (a greppable mention;
 a numbered list item has no anchor to link to), not its
 number. Long-tail entries
 live in [todo-backlog.md](todo-backlog.md). Use the
 [Prose Form in AGENTS.md](../AGENTS.md#prose-form); deeper
 detail goes in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` design
 subsections (link via `[N]` ref).


## Done

Completed tasks are moved from `## Todo` to here, `## Done`, as they are completed
and older `## Done` sections are moved to [done.md](done.md) to keep this file small.


# References
